children.name: Alauda
children.description: The Skylark's sustained spiralling song-flight — rising to a tiny dot 300 m above Swedish farmland — is one of the most celebrated sounds in northern European natural history. Populations have fallen by over half across Europe in 50 years due to changes in farming.

children.name: Alauda arvensis
children.description: Its sustained, rippling song delivered from a hovering speck high above the fields is one of the most celebrated sounds of the European farmland summer. Populations have more than halved in Europe in fifty years due to intensive agricultural practices.

children.name: Alauda gulgula
children.description: The Oriental Skylark occupies open grasslands, cultivation, and scrubby hillsides from the Middle East east through the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It closely resembles the Eurasian Skylark but tends to have a shorter crest and different song phrasing.

children.name: Alauda razae
children.description: Critically endangered and confined to the tiny, arid islet of Raso in Cape Verde, the Raso Lark has one of the most restricted ranges of any bird species in the world. The entire population fluctuates between fewer than 250 and roughly 1,500 individuals depending on rainfall-driven food availability.

children.name: Alauda japonica
children.description: Formerly treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian Skylark, the Japanese Skylark breeds on the open grasslands and agricultural plains of Japan and northeastern China. Its song-flight display is essentially identical to that of arvensis, and it winters in the lowlands of East Asia.

children.name: Lullula
children.description: A monotypic genus of sandy open country and heath, the Woodlark is distinguished from Alauda by its very short tail, prominent supercilia meeting at the bill, and a liquid, cascading song considered among the finest of any European passerine.

children.name: Lullula arborea
children.description: A short-tailed lark with a sweet, yodelling, downward-cascading song, favouring sandy heaths and clearings with scattered trees. It reaches the northern edge of its range in southern Sweden and winters in the Mediterranean basin and North Africa.

children.name: Eremophila
children.description: Larks with striking black-and-yellow facial patterns and small feathered 'horns', breeding on open tundra, steppe, and desert plateaus across the Holarctic. The genus shows remarkable geographic variation, with dozens of subspecies adapted to different climatic zones.

children.name: Eremophila alpestris
children.description: A lark with a striking black-and-yellow face pattern and tiny feathered 'horns', breeding on Arctic and alpine tundra across the Northern Hemisphere. Swedish birds frequent the high fells, with wintering flocks gathering on coastal shores and farmland.

children.name: Eremophila bilopha
children.description: Temminck's Lark replaces the Horned Lark in stony desert and semi-arid scrub across North Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. It is distinguished by its entirely black forehead, longer horn tufts, and preference for gravelly desert plains.

children.name: Melanocorypha
children.description: Large, robust larks of open steppe and desert from the Iberian Peninsula east across Central Asia to Mongolia and China. Many species are partly or wholly migratory, and several are characterised by conspicuous white or black-tipped wing patterns visible in flight.

children.name: Melanocorypha calandra
children.description: A large, heavy-billed lark of Mediterranean and Eurasian steppes, notable for its loud, varied song incorporating mimicry of other species. Large black neck patches are diagnostic, and the wings show distinctive white trailing edges in flight.

children.name: Melanocorypha bimaculata
children.description: The Bimaculated Lark breeds on dry, rocky hillsides and semi-desert from Turkey east to Central Asia, wintering on the Indian subcontinent and northeast Africa. It closely resembles the Calandra Lark but has a shorter tail and more extensive dark breast patches.

children.name: Melanocorypha mongolica
children.description: The Mongolian Lark is a large, striking lark of the Mongolian and Chinese steppes, characterised by a white supercilium and white underparts with a dark chestband. It is largely sedentary or partially migratory within East Asia.

children.name: Melanocorypha leucoptera
children.description: As its name implies, the White-winged Lark has extensive white on the secondaries, making it one of the most distinctive larks in flight when its breeding range on the Kazakh and Russian steppes gives way to wintering grounds further south and west.

children.name: Melanocorypha yeltoniensis
children.description: The Black Lark is unique among larks for the male's almost entirely black plumage, contrasting with the streaked brown female. It inhabits grassy and wormwood steppes east of the Caspian Sea and winters in small flocks on the Central Asian plains.

children.name: Melanocorypha maxima
children.description: The largest lark in the world, the Tibetan Lark inhabits high-altitude grasslands and marshes on the Tibetan Plateau and adjacent mountains. Its heavy bill, large body, and slow, powerful flight set it apart from all other larks.

children.name: Calandrella
children.description: Small, compact short-toed larks of arid and semi-arid open country across the Palearctic, Middle East, and East Africa. The genus includes several cryptic species, best separated by voice, range, and subtle structural features.

children.name: Calandrella brachydactyla
children.description: A small pale lark of dry, open habitats from southern Europe and North Africa east to Central Asia, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. It is identified by its pale sandy plumage, very short hind claw, and a small dark patch at the side of the breast.

children.name: Calandrella acutirostris
children.description: Hume's Short-toed Lark breeds on high-altitude rocky plateaus from Afghanistan through the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau, generally at elevations above those tolerated by the Greater Short-toed Lark. It winters on open plains of the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Calandrella cheleensis
children.description: The Asian Short-toed Lark occupies dry grasslands, steppe, and semi-desert across Central and East Asia. It is closely related to the Greater Short-toed Lark but has a heavier bill, more streaked upperparts, and a more easterly distribution.

children.name: Calandrella blanfordi
children.description: Blanford's Short-toed Lark inhabits semi-arid scrub and grasslands on the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, with a range extending into Ethiopia and Somalia. It is a rather plain, sandy-coloured lark with a relatively strong bill.

children.name: Calandrella somalica
children.description: The Somali Short-toed Lark is endemic to the Horn of Africa, inhabiting dry, open grasslands and bush in Somalia, Ethiopia, and northern Kenya. It is distinguished from related species by its slightly smaller size and different call.

children.name: Calandrella erlangeri
children.description: Erlanger's Lark is a little-known short-toed lark of dry open plains in the Horn of Africa, mainly recorded in Somalia and Ethiopia. It was named in honour of the German ornithologist Carl von Erlanger, who collected the type specimen in the early 20th century.

children.name: Calandrella cinerea
children.description: The Red-capped Lark is a neat, small lark of short-grass plains and bare ground throughout sub-Saharan Africa, named for the rufous cap and chestnut shoulder patches that ornament otherwise sandy-brown plumage. It forages actively on the ground and breeds year-round in equatorial regions.

children.name: Calandrella rufescens
children.description: The Lesser Short-toed Lark is a small, heavily streaked lark of saltflats, steppe, and semi-desert from the Canary Islands and Iberian Peninsula east across Central Asia to China, wintering in North and East Africa. It is more streaked above than the Greater Short-toed Lark and has a smaller dark breast patch.

children.name: Calendulauda
children.description: African bush-larks and related savanna and scrubland species, largely confined to sub-Saharan Africa and the southern half of the continent. Many members are skulking birds of dense vegetation, detected more easily by song than by sight.

children.name: Calendulauda africanoides
children.description: The Fawn-colored Lark is a widespread resident of dry thornbush and open woodland in eastern and southern Africa, with pale, sandy plumage that provides camouflage on bare ground and leaf litter. It has a melodious song delivered from a low perch or in short song-flights.

children.name: Calendulauda albescens
children.description: The Karoo Lark is endemic to the Karoo semi-desert and Namaqualand of South Africa and Namibia, where it occupies dry, stony scrublands. Pale and streaked, it is closely associated with the succulent Karoo biome and largely sedentary throughout its range.

children.name: Calendulauda alopex
children.description: The Athi Short-toed Lark is a small, pale lark of dry grasslands and short-grass plains in East Africa, ranging from southern Ethiopia through Kenya to Tanzania. It inhabits open, often overgrazed, areas and is associated with arid savanna conditions.

children.name: Calendulauda burra
children.description: The Red Lark is a range-restricted endemic of the red sand dunes of Bushmanland in South Africa's Northern Cape, where its rich rufous-orange plumage matches the substrate perfectly. It is Near Threatened due to its tiny range and vulnerability to habitat alteration.

children.name: Calendulauda erythrochlamys
children.description: Rudd's Lark is a secretive, threatened lark restricted to montane grasslands in eastern South Africa and the highlands of Lesotho. It is the rarest lark in southern Africa, with a small, fragmented population vulnerable to overgrazing and the loss of its natural grassland habitat.

children.name: Calendulauda poecilosterna
children.description: The Pink-breasted Lark is a slender, long-tailed lark of dry bush and scrubby grasslands in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Somalia. Its distinctive pink-washed underparts are unique among African larks, and it is typically found in pairs on sandy, lightly vegetated soils.

children.name: Calendulauda sabota
children.description: The Sabota Lark is one of the commonest larks of dry savanna in southern and eastern Africa, with a rich, varied song that includes extensive mimicry of other species. It inhabits open broad-leaved woodland and thornbush, often in rocky areas, and is largely resident.

children.name: Calendulauda seebohmi
children.description: Barlow's Lark is a recently recognised species of coastal Namibia, separated from the Karoo Lark on the basis of plumage, song, and habitat differences. It inhabits dry, stony scrub on gravel plains close to the Atlantic coast.

children.name: Eremalauda
children.description: Small desert larks of sandy and stony wastes in North Africa, the Sahara-Sahel zone, and the Arabian Peninsula. Often pale and cryptically coloured, they are adapted to extreme aridity with a preference for sparse, wind-rippled sand surfaces.

children.name: Eremalauda dunni
children.description: Dunn's Lark is a small, very pale lark of sandy desert and semi-desert from Mauritania and Mali east through Sudan and the Arabian Peninsula. Its almost unmarked, cream-white plumage renders it nearly invisible on pale desert sands.

children.name: Eremalauda starki
children.description: Stark's Lark inhabits open sandy desert and dry savanna in Namibia and Botswana, favouring areas of sparse grass on pale sandy soils where it feeds on seeds. It is nomadic after rain events, appearing in large flocks when conditions are suitable.

children.name: Spizocorys
children.description: Small, short-billed larks of dry grassland, steppe, and semi-arid scrub in Africa, characterised by compact build and often heavily streaked plumage. Several species are Near Threatened or restricted to the Horn of Africa.

children.name: Spizocorys conirostris
children.description: The Pink-billed Lark is a widespread small lark of open grassland and dry savanna in southern Africa, named for its distinctive pale pinkish cone-shaped bill. It forms nomadic flocks outside the breeding season, congregating in areas of short grass after rainfall.

children.name: Spizocorys fringillaris
children.description: Botha's Lark is one of southern Africa's rarest and most threatened birds, restricted to a tiny area of specialised high-altitude grasslands in the eastern Free State and Mpumalanga provinces of South Africa. Overgrazing and agricultural conversion have reduced its already minuscule range critically.

children.name: Spizocorys obbiensis
children.description: The Obbia Lark is a poorly known, Near Threatened species endemic to a small coastal area of central Somalia around the port of Obbia (Hobyo), where it occupies flat, sandy coastal scrub. It is one of the least-studied larks in Africa.

children.name: Spizocorys personata
children.description: The Masked Lark is a small, sandy-brown lark of dry grasslands and semi-arid bush in Ethiopia, Somalia, and Kenya. The subspecies vary in the extent of facial markings, which give the bird a masked appearance through diffuse dark streaking around the face.

children.name: Spizocorys sclateri
children.description: Sclater's Lark is a range-restricted species of stony plains in the arid Northern Cape and southern Namibia of southern Africa. It is named after Philip Lutley Sclater, the prominent Victorian zoologist and Secretary of the Zoological Society of London.

children.name: Galerida
children.description: Crested larks with prominent, always-erect crests, widespread from Western Europe and North Africa across the Middle East and South Asia to Southeast Asia. They favour open, often disturbed habitats including roadsides, cultivation margins, and semi-desert, and are largely sedentary.

children.name: Galerida cristata
children.description: The Crested Lark is the quintessential roadside lark of Europe and western Asia, recognisable by its long, pointed crest and streaked sandy-brown plumage. It is a familiar sight along roadsides, on bare agricultural land, and in villages, uttering a rich, fluty song.

children.name: Galerida theklae
children.description: Thekla's Lark replaces the Crested Lark on rocky hillsides and stony scrub in Iberia, North Africa, and the Sahara-Sahel zone. It has a shorter, fuller crest and a heavier bill than the Crested Lark and tends to favour stonier, less disturbed habitats.

children.name: Galerida malabarica
children.description: The Malabar Lark is endemic to peninsular India, where it inhabits open scrub, rocky ground, and cultivation margins along the western Ghats and the Deccan Plateau. It is closely related to the Crested Lark but occupies a fully allopatric range on the subcontinent.

children.name: Galerida deva
children.description: Sykes's Lark is a small crested lark of the dry scrub and rocky plateaus of peninsular India, from Gujarat south to Tamil Nadu. It is smaller and paler than the Malabar Lark, with a shorter bill and slightly different song.

children.name: Galerida magnirostris
children.description: The Large-billed Lark is a robust crested lark of rocky fynbos, Renosterveld, and stony Karoo scrub in southern South Africa and Lesotho. Its large, heavy bill distinguishes it from all other crested larks on the continent.

children.name: Galerida modesta
children.description: The Sun Lark is a small crested lark of rocky outcrops, laterite escarpments, and savanna in West and Central Africa from Guinea east to Sudan and Ethiopia. It is unobtrusive and rather plain, but delivers a distinctive musical song from prominent perches.

children.name: Mirafra
children.description: The largest genus of larks, comprising African and Asian bush-larks of open woodland, savanna, and grassland. Many Mirafra larks are best identified by their wing-snapping display flights and elaborate, musical songs; plumage is often cryptically variable.

children.name: Mirafra africana
children.description: The Rufous-naped Lark is one of the most widespread larks in sub-Saharan Africa, occupying moist and dry savanna from Sudan and Ethiopia south to South Africa. Males perform a conspicuous display flight, clapping the wings loudly over the back while singing a clear, whistling song.

children.name: Mirafra angolensis
children.description: The Angolan Lark is a medium-sized bush-lark of the Miombo woodland zone in Angola, Zambia, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. It is closely related to the Rufous-naped Lark and similarly performs wing-snapping display flights.

children.name: Mirafra apiata
children.description: The Clapper Lark is named for the loud wing-clapping it produces during its aerial display, a sound that carries considerable distance over the dry Fynbos, Renosterveld, and open scrub it inhabits in southwestern and southern Africa.

children.name: Mirafra ashi
children.description: Ash's Lark is a Vulnerable species known only from a tiny area of coastal Somalia around Mogadishu, where it inhabits sandy semi-desert with sparse scrub. It is named after John Salter Ash, a British ornithologist who worked extensively in Ethiopia and Somalia.

children.name: Mirafra cantillans
children.description: The Singing Bush Lark is a small lark of open grasslands, cultivation edges, and dry scrub from the Middle East east to India and south to East Africa. Males deliver an extended, melodious song from a low perch or in a slow, circling song-flight.

children.name: Mirafra cheniana
children.description: The Melodious Lark is a migratory lark of southern African grasslands, breeding in short grassland in South Africa and Zimbabwe and spending the non-breeding season in East Africa. It is a gifted mimic, incorporating phrases from many other bird species into its rich, varied song.

children.name: Mirafra cordofanica
children.description: The Kordofan Lark is a lark of sandy semi-desert and open acacia savanna in the Sahel zone, ranging from Mauritania east to Sudan and Eritrea. It is named after the Kordofan region of Sudan, where it is a characteristic bird of the gum arabic belt.

children.name: Mirafra fasciolata
children.description: The Eastern Clapper Lark is a common bush-lark of dry grassland, open savanna, and rocky terrain in eastern and southern Africa, closely related to M. apiata. It performs the same dramatic wing-clapping display flight but tends to inhabit more arid and rocky areas.

children.name: Mirafra javanica
children.description: The Australasian Lark has the most easterly distribution of any Mirafra, reaching Australia, where it inhabits open grasslands and dry savanna across the north and interior. This distribution makes it the only lark regularly recorded in Australasia.

children.name: Mirafra albicauda
children.description: The White-tailed Lark is a grassland specialist of moist, medium-height grasslands in western Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania. It is characterised by conspicuous white outer tail feathers and a skulking behaviour that makes it difficult to observe in dense grass.

children.name: Mirafra williamsi
children.description: Williams's Lark is a rarely observed lark restricted to arid sandy plains and sparse scrub in northern Kenya, first described in 1955. It is named after J.G. Williams, a pioneering ornithologist and author of field guides to East African birds.

children.name: Mirafra somalica
children.description: The Somali Long-billed Lark is a distinctive lark of the Ethiopian and Somalian Highlands, characterised by a long, slightly decurved bill used to probe dry soil and leaf litter. It inhabits open, rocky hillsides and short-grass areas at moderate elevations.

children.name: Mirafra hypermetra
children.description: The Red-winged Lark is a large, imposing lark of open savanna and grassy plains in East Africa, from Ethiopia south to Tanzania. In flight, the broadly rufous wings are highly conspicuous, making it one of the most striking larks on the continent.

children.name: Mirafra rufocinnamomea
children.description: The Flappet Lark is one of the most widespread bush-larks in sub-Saharan Africa, named for the distinctive flapping-clapping sound of its wing-snapping display flight. It inhabits open, dry deciduous woodland and scrubby grassland from Senegal east to Ethiopia and south to Zimbabwe.

children.name: Mirafra pulpa
children.description: Friedmann's Lark is a localised and little-known species of semi-arid scrub and bush in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Somalia. It was named in honour of Herbert Friedmann, the American ornithologist known for his work on African birds and brood parasitism.

children.name: Mirafra hova
children.description: The Madagascar Lark is the only lark found on Madagascar, inhabiting open grasslands and cultivated areas across much of the island. Its presence so far from continental Africa makes it biogeographically exceptional among the family.

children.name: Mirafra rufa
children.description: The Rusty Bush Lark is a warmly coloured, rufous-tinged lark of grassy savanna and open bush in the Sahel and northern East Africa. It is similar to the Flappet Lark but tends to be more rufous overall and is found in rather drier habitats.

children.name: Mirafra affinis
children.description: Jerdon's Bush Lark is a small, streaked lark of open grasslands, dry cultivation, and rocky hillsides in peninsular India and Sri Lanka. It was named after Thomas C. Jerdon, the British army surgeon and naturalist who extensively documented Indian birds in the 19th century.

children.name: Mirafra passerina
children.description: The Monotonous Lark is aptly named for the male's persistent, repeated single-note song, delivered from a perch or in a circling song-flight over dry savanna and open scrub in southern Africa. It is a Kalahari basin specialist, most common in Botswana and Namibia.

children.name: Mirafra erythroptera
children.description: The Indian Bush Lark is a medium-sized, rufous-winged lark of open scrub, dry grassland, and cultivation across the Indian subcontinent. In flight, the chestnut wing-panel is conspicuous, distinguishing it from most other South Asian larks.

children.name: Mirafra gilletti
children.description: Gillett's Lark is a sparsely recorded lark of semi-arid scrub and dry grasslands in Ethiopia and Somalia. It was named after J.B. Gillett, a British botanist who collected the type specimen while working in the Horn of Africa.

children.name: Mirafra degodiensis
children.description: The Degodi Lark is a rare and poorly known species restricted to the Degodi scrublands of southern Ethiopia, discovered and described in the 1970s. It inhabits sandy soils with sparse acacia scrub and is considered Vulnerable due to its extremely limited range.

children.name: Mirafra collaris
children.description: The Collared Lark is a distinctive, Near Threatened lark of sandy and stony semi-desert in the Horn of Africa, characterised by a broad dark collar across the lower throat and breast. Its range is centred on northeastern Kenya, Somalia, and the Ogaden region of Ethiopia.

children.name: Mirafra harterti
children.description: Hartert's Lark is an enigmatic, little-known species from dry scrub in Somalia and adjacent areas of Ethiopia, named after the distinguished German ornithologist Ernst Hartert of the Tring Museum. It is known from very few specimens and its exact taxonomic status has been debated.

children.name: Mirafra alopex
children.description: The Abyssinian Lark is a medium-sized bush-lark of the Ethiopian Highlands and the East African Rift Valley region, where it inhabits open grassland, stony hillsides, and lightly wooded savanna at moderate to high altitudes. It is closely related to the Rufous-naped Lark but found at higher elevations.

children.name: Mirafra naevia
children.description: The Nyasa Lark is a small, heavily streaked bush-lark of open miombo woodland and bracken-briar grassland around Lake Malawi and adjacent Zambia and Mozambique. It is associated with damper, higher-altitude grassland than most related Mirafra species.

children.name: Mirafra poecilosterna
children.description: The Pink-breasted Bush Lark is a pale, slender lark of dry acacia scrub and open bush in the Horn of Africa and northeastern Kenya. It is one of several similar species in the region that are best separated by voice and exact locality.

children.name: Mirafra fischeri
children.description: Fischer's Lark is a lark of open dry grassland and acacia bush in East Africa, from Ethiopia south to Tanzania. It was named after the German explorer and naturalist Gustav Fischer, who collected natural history specimens during expeditions to East Africa in the 1880s.

children.name: Mirafra albicaudatus
children.description: The Somali Bush Lark is a small, pale lark of dry acacia scrub and sandy semi-desert in Somalia and the Ogaden region of Ethiopia. It is one of several endemic or near-endemic lark species found in the Horn of Africa, a hotspot for lark diversity.

children.name: Mirafra torrida
children.description: The Kilimanjaro Lark is a little-known lark of open grasslands at moderate elevations in northern Tanzania and adjacent Kenya, in the zone around Mount Kilimanjaro and the Arusha region. It was previously treated as a subspecies of the Rufous-naped Lark.

children.name: Mirafra levaillantii
children.description: The Natal Lark is a secretive, grassland specialist of moist tall grassland in eastern South Africa, Lesotho, and Swaziland, named in honour of François Le Vaillant, the pioneering French naturalist and ornithologist who explored southern Africa in the late 18th century.

children.name: Mirafra burra
children.description: The Degodi Bush Lark is a poorly known species of dry scrub in the Degodi district of southern Ethiopia, closely related to other Mirafra bush-larks of the Horn of Africa. It inhabits sparse acacia scrub on sandy and rocky soils and is considered rare.

children.name: Mirafra marjoriae
children.description: Barlow's Bush Lark is a sparsely known lark of the arid scrublands of Namibia's interior, named after Marjorie Barlow. It was separated from related taxa based on differences in song, plumage tone, and microhabitat preference in gravel-plain and scrubby savanna.

children.name: Mirafra sharpii
children.description: Sharpe's Lark is a poorly known Mirafra of dry open bush and scrubby grassland in the Horn of Africa, named after the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe who catalogued vast numbers of bird specimens at the Natural History Museum in London.

children.name: Chersomanes
children.description: A monotypic genus of robust, long-billed larks of dry scrub and stony plains in southern Africa, close in ecology to the long-billed Certhilauda larks.

children.name: Chersomanes albofasciata
children.description: The Spike-heeled Lark is a distinctive lark of dry stony plains, Karoo scrub, and open savanna in southern Africa, named for its exceptionally elongated hind claw (the 'spike'). It runs rapidly across the ground and tends to stand very upright, giving it a jaunty appearance.

children.name: Pinarocorys
children.description: Medium-sized, rather thrush-like African larks of dry open woodland and savanna, less closely associated with bare ground than most larks and more often found in lightly wooded areas.

children.name: Pinarocorys erythropygia
children.description: The Rufous-rumped Lark is an intra-African migrant breeding in Sahel-zone acacia scrub and woodland, where its rufous rump and uppertail coverts are visible in flight. It winters further south in the Guinea savanna zone and is usually seen singly on the ground under sparse cover.

children.name: Pinarocorys nigricans
children.description: The Dusky Lark is a large, dark, thrush-like lark of miombo woodland and open savanna in central and southern Africa. An intra-African migrant, it breeds in southern Africa and moves north to wintering grounds in Tanzania and the DRC.

children.name: Ramphocoris
children.description: A monotypic genus of very large-billed larks confined to stony desert and rocky semi-desert across North Africa and the Arabian Peninsula.

children.name: Ramphocoris clotbey
children.description: The Thick-billed Lark is a large, striking lark of stony and gravelly desert from the Western Sahara and Morocco east through Libya, Egypt, and the Sinai to the Arabian Peninsula. Its enormous, almost finch-like bill is used to crack hard seeds and tough invertebrate prey unavailable to other larks.

children.name: Ammomanes
children.description: Desert finch-larks and desert larks of sandy and rocky deserts from the Sahara across the Arabian Peninsula to South Asia, characterised by plain sandy or rufous plumage and unstreaked underparts that blend with their arid substrates.

children.name: Ammomanes cinctura
children.description: The Bar-tailed Lark inhabits stony and sandy deserts from the Sahara and Canary Islands east to Pakistan and northwestern India, named for the narrow black subterminal bar on the tail. It is pale and unstreaked, blending seamlessly with the pale gravel and sandy substrates it prefers.

children.name: Ammomanes deserti
children.description: The Desert Lark is one of the most widespread desert-dwelling birds in the world, ranging from the Atlantic coast of Morocco east across North Africa and the Arabian Peninsula to Pakistan. Its plumage varies geographically from pale sandy-grey to rich tawny-rufous, matching local rock and soil colours.

children.name: Ammomanes phoenicura
children.description: The Rufous-tailed Lark inhabits rocky hillsides, stony plateaus, and eroded ravines across peninsular India, where its rich rufous tail contrasts with its otherwise sandy-buff plumage. It often perches conspicuously on boulders and has a clear, whistling song.

children.name: Ammomanes azizi
children.description: The Saharan Desert Lark is a recently elevated species of central Saharan rock desert, previously treated as a subspecies of Ammomanes deserti. It occupies the rocky regs and hamadas of Algeria, Libya, and Chad, where its pale sandy-grey plumage is an excellent match to the substrate.

children.name: Ammomanopsis
children.description: A monotypic genus closely related to Ammomanes, confined to the gravel plains and stony desert of Namibia and southern Angola. Previously included within Ammomanes, it is now recognised as a separate genus on the basis of molecular and morphological data.

children.name: Ammomanopsis grayi
children.description: Gray's Lark is a very pale, lightly built lark of Namibia's gravel plains and the coastal Namib Desert, where it forages for seeds and small invertebrates on open, flat terrain. It is uniquely adapted to the hyper-arid conditions of the central Namib and Hartmann Valley.

children.name: Chersophilus
children.description: A monotypic genus of Mediterranean and North African semi-deserts, closely allied to Galerida but differing in structure, song, and habitat preferences. Dupont's Lark is highly secretive and more often heard than seen.

children.name: Chersophilus duponti
children.description: Dupont's Lark is a secretive, declining species of flat, open steppe with scattered low scrub in central Spain and North Africa. Its eerie, far-carrying nocturnal song is far easier to hear than the bird is to see; it runs rapidly through vegetation and is reluctant to fly.

children.name: Heteromirafra
children.description: Rare and threatened larks of specialised grassland habitats in eastern Africa and South Africa, closely related to Mirafra but separable by molecular data and highly restricted ranges. Several species have experienced significant population declines.

children.name: Heteromirafra archeri
children.description: Archer's Lark is a critically endangered species known from only a tiny area of short-grass plains in the Hobyo-Mudug region of Somalia; it may already be extinct. Named after Geoffrey Francis Bertram Archer, a British colonial official and ornithologist who worked in Somalia.

children.name: Heteromirafra ruddi
children.description: Rudd's Lark is a critically endangered grassland specialist restricted to a few patches of high-altitude grassland in South Africa's eastern highveld and adjacent Swaziland. It has suffered severe population declines due to loss and degradation of its very specialised habitat.

children.name: Heteromirafra sidamoensis
children.description: The Sidamo Lark is a critically endangered lark found only in a small area of short-grass plains in the Liben Plain of southern Ethiopia, where it may number fewer than 100 individuals. It is considered one of Africa's most threatened birds and faces extinction from overgrazing and agricultural expansion.

children.name: Certhilauda
children.description: Long-billed larks of southern African arid and semi-arid zones, with slender, decurved bills used to probe sandy soil and rock crevices for invertebrates. Several species are endemic to the Karoo, Namaqualand, or coastal Namibia.

children.name: Certhilauda chuana
children.description: The Short-clawed Lark is a large, long-billed lark of dry Kalahari sandveld, thornbush, and open woodland in Botswana and the Northern Cape of South Africa. It forages by probing sandy soils for beetle larvae and other invertebrates with its slightly decurved bill.

children.name: Certhilauda curvirostris
children.description: The Cape Long-billed Lark is the most widespread member of the genus, inhabiting stony Karoo scrub, Renosterveld, and fynbos margins in the Western and Northern Cape of South Africa. Its strongly decurved bill is used to probe crevices in rocky ground.

children.name: Certhilauda subcoronata
children.description: The Karoo Long-billed Lark inhabits flat and gently rolling Karoo semi-desert in South Africa and southern Namibia, where it probes bare soil and gravel for insects. It is streaked brown above with pale underparts and has a moderately curved bill.

children.name: Certhilauda semitorquata
children.description: The Eastern Long-billed Lark inhabits stony hillsides and escarpments in eastern South Africa and Lesotho, where it is most common on rocky slopes with sparse grass and scattered boulders. A partial dark necklace across the breast distinguishes it from related species.

children.name: Certhilauda barlowi
children.description: Barlow's Lark is a recently described long-billed lark endemic to the coastal Namib Desert and inland gravel plains of southern Namibia. It was separated from related taxa on the basis of differences in bill shape, plumage, and vocalisation.

children.name: Alaudala
children.description: Small, compact short-toed larks split from Calandrella on the basis of molecular phylogenetics, distributed across the Palearctic from the Mediterranean to Central Asia and South Asia. Many members of this genus were previously subsumed within Calandrella brachydactyla or C. rufescens.

children.name: Alaudala heinei
children.description: Heine's Short-toed Lark is a small, pale lark of semi-arid steppe and desert margins in Central Asia, previously treated as a subspecies of the Lesser Short-toed Lark. It was named in honour of Ferdinand Heine, the German ornithologist and co-author of an important 19th-century bird catalogue.

children.name: Alaudala rufescens
children.description: The Lesser Short-toed Lark is a small, heavily streaked lark of saltflats, coastal dunes, and semi-desert across the western Palearctic, from the Canary Islands and Iberian Peninsula east across Central Asia. It is more heavily streaked than the Greater Short-toed Lark and tends to favour saltier, damper substrates.

children.name: Alaudala raytal
children.description: The Sand Lark is a small, pale lark closely associated with sandy riverbeds and sandbanks along large rivers in the Indian subcontinent and Myanmar. It is one of the few larks with a strong association with sandy alluvial habitats rather than arid desert.

children.name: Alaudala minor
children.description: The Saharan Short-toed Lark inhabits stony desert, desert margins, and arid scrub across the central and eastern Sahara from Algeria and Libya east to Sudan. It is one of several cryptic short-toed larks of North Africa that were long subsumed within broader species concepts.
